ground_truth_value: "QuoteKG is the first multilingual knowledge graph of quotes, designed to capture the quotes of public figures across languages and contexts. It is generated through a semi-automated pipeline that extracts quotes from Wikiquote  aligns multilingual variations, and connects them to entities in Wikidata and DBpedia. The dataset contains nearly one million quotes in 55 languages, associated with more than 69,000 public figures, and includes over 38,000 cross-lingual mentions and 13,000 misattributed quotations. Each quote is enriched with contextual metadata, allowing users to explore relationships between quotations, speakers, and themes across time and cultures. QuoteKG provides a foundation for research in digital humanities, natural language processing, knowledge extraction, and cross-lingual semantic alignment."
user_value: "QuoteKG is a large-scale multilingual knowledge graph of quotations, designed to capture and link quotes from public figures across different languages, contexts, and sources. Formally defined as a DCAT dataset and VoID dataset (http://www.w3.org/ns/dcat#Dataset, http://rdfs.org/ns/void#Dataset), QuoteKG represents the first structured effort to semantically model quotations at scale. Its schema conforms to the RDF syntax model (http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#)."

"QuoteKG is the first multilingual knowledge graph of quotes, designed to capture the quotes of public figures across languages and contexts. It is generated through a semi-automated pipeline that extracts quotes from Wikiquote  aligns multilingual variations, and connects them to entities in Wikidata and DBpedia. The dataset contains nearly one million quotes in 55 languages, associated with more than 69,000 public figures, and includes over 38,000 cross-lingual mentions and 13,000 misattributed quotations. Each quote is enriched with contextual metadata, allowing users to explore relationships between quotations, speakers, and themes across time and cultures. QuoteKG provides a foundation for research in digital humanities, natural language processing, knowledge extraction, and cross-lingual semantic alignment."
